findings.description: The PDF presents data in a compact, dense tabular format across the full page width. The HTML conversion spreads the table into a much more vertically expanded layout with significantly more whitespace between rows, changing the overall page structure substantially.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows the entire first table (1997-2002) fitting on one page. HTML spreads this same content across much more vertical space with larger row heights.

findings.description: Table header structure differs slightly - PDF uses inline column headers (Collection/amount/[$]) while HTML uses multi-line header cells with 'Fiscal Year XXXX Collection amount [$]' format, but data content is preserved.
findings.evidence: PDF headers show 'Collection', 'amount', '[$]' as separate sub-rows under year headers. HTML combines these into single header cells like 'Fiscal Year 1997-1998 Collection amount [$]'.

findings.description: Same header formatting difference applies to the second table (2002-2007) on page 2.
findings.evidence: PDF shows compact column headers vs HTML's expanded multi-line header format.

findings.description: Page 3 in PDF contains the continuation table (2007-2012+) plus extensive footnotes all on one page. The HTML splits this into significantly more vertical space.
findings.evidence: PDF page 3 has table and all footnotes fitting in a single dense page. HTML spreads these across much more space.

findings.description: Footnote text formatting differs - PDF has underlined dates (e.g., 'May 1, 1999', 'September 1, 2009') while HTML also appears to preserve underlining but with slightly different text flow.
findings.evidence: PDF footnotes use underlined dates like 'May 1, 1999'. HTML appears to preserve this formatting.

findings.description: The 2011-2012+ column header in HTML shows '2011-2012+' correctly matching the PDF, data integrity preserved.
findings.evidence: Both PDF and HTML show the '+' notation on the final fiscal year column.

findings.description: The HTML uses nested <main> elements - one in the body and one inside it. The subtitle '[§ 105 ARTICLE 2C.]' is rendered as h2 which is acceptable but could be considered part of the h1 title rather than a separate section heading.
findings.evidence:
HTML has

TABLE 12. NET ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGE TAX COLLECTIONS BY TYPE

followed by

[§ 105 ARTICLE 2C.]

. In the PDF, this is a subtitle under the main title.

findings.description: The PDF page 1 shows partial data for fiscal year 2001-2002 in the first table, with some values cut off at the right edge. The HTML includes full 2001-2002 values. While many match the visible portions, some values in the PDF are truncated and cannot be fully verified. However, the values that are visible appear consistent.
findings.evidence: PDF shows truncated columns for 2001-2002 (e.g., Beer excise shows '88' truncated, HTML shows '88,631,573'). The HTML values appear to be correctly extrapolated from the visible data in the PDF.

findings.description: For fiscal year 2001-2002, the local distribution allocations cell shows '-' in the HTML, but the PDF shows no dash - it appears to be blank or the value is not visible due to column truncation. The special reserve fund for 2001-2002 shows 26,690,051 in both the PDF and HTML, which is consistent with the footnote.
findings.evidence: HTML: Local distribution allocations 2001-2002 = '-', '-'. The PDF's 2001-2002 column is partially cut off but the footnote confirms the $26,690,051 was transferred to special reserve fund instead of distributed locally.

findings.description: The HTML contains two nested <main> elements, which is invalid HTML structure but does not cause content duplication.
findings.evidence: HTML has <main> wrapping the entire body content, then another <main> inside it wrapping the tables and footnotes.
